questions.question: Explain the concept of upcasting and downcasting with an example.
keyPoints: Upcasting: Converting derived class pointer/reference to base class pointer/reference
Always safe and implicit in C++
Downcasting: Converting base class pointer/reference to derived class pointer/reference
Requires explicit cast and is unsafe
dynamic_cast provides runtime type checking
Example: Base *b = new Derived(); // upcasting

questions.question: What is the Virtual Function Table (vtable)? Explain how virtual function calls work at runtime.
keyPoints: vtable is a runtime data structure for each class with virtual functions
Contains pointers to virtual function implementations
Each object has a hidden vptr pointing to class vtable
When virtual function is called, vptr→vtable→function address is looked up
This enables dynamic binding based on actual object type
Compiler generates this automatically for classes with virtual functions

questions.question: What is object slicing? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Object slicing occurs when derived object is assigned to base object by value
The derived portion is 'sliced off', losing polymorphic behavior
Only base class data members are copied
Prevention: Use pointers or references to base class
Alternative: Use clone() or virtual copy constructor pattern
Example: Base b = Derived(); // slicing happens

questions.question: Explain the difference between early binding and late binding with examples.
keyPoints: Early binding (static): Function call resolved at compile time
Based on type of pointer/reference, not object type
Non-virtual functions use early binding
Late binding (dynamic): Function call resolved at runtime
Based on actual object type pointed to
Virtual functions use late binding
Example: Base *p = new Derived(); p->show(); // late if virtual

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'override' specifier in C++11? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Ensures derived class function overrides a base virtual function
Compiler generates error if no matching virtual function exists
Helps catch typos and signature mismatches
Example: void f() override { } // must match base virtual f()
Prevents accidental overloading instead of overriding
Makes code more readable and maintainable

questions.question: What is a pure virtual function? How does it differ from a virtual function?
keyPoints: Pure virtual function declared with '= 0' syntax
Has no implementation in base class
Must be overridden by derived classes
Class with pure virtual function becomes abstract
Cannot instantiate abstract class
Regular virtual function has implementation in base class
Derived class may override but not mandatory

questions.question: Explain the concept of runtime polymorphism using pointers to base class.
keyPoints: Base class pointer can hold address of derived class object
When virtual function is called, derived version is invoked
Same pointer can point to different derived objects at runtime
Enables writing generic code that works with multiple derived types
Achieved through virtual functions and dynamic binding
Example: Base *ptr; ptr = &obj1; ptr->func(); // calls Derived1::func()

questions.question: Create a complete C++ program demonstrating polymorphism using pointers to base and derived classes. Include virtual functions, virtual destructor, and explain the output at each step.
keyPoints: Define base class with virtual function and virtual destructor
Define derived classes overriding virtual function
Create base pointer pointing to derived objects
Demonstrate polymorphism with multiple derived types
Show that without virtual, base function is called
With virtual, derived function is called based on object type
Virtual destructor ensures proper cleanup order
Use dynamic_cast for safe downcasting
Compile and explain each output

questions.question: Design an abstract class Shape with pure virtual function area(). Create derived classes Circle and Rectangle that implement area(). Write a main function demonstrating polymorphism using base class pointer.
keyPoints: Define abstract class Shape with protected members
Pure virtual function: virtual double area() = 0;
Circle class with radius and circle area calculation
Rectangle class with length, breadth and rectangle area calculation
Main function creates array of Shape pointers
Demonstrate polymorphism: same pointer calls different area()
Proper use of override specifier
Virtual destructor for proper cleanup
Show encapsulation and abstraction principles

questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual destructors. Write a program to demonstrate why virtual destructors are necessary when using base class pointers to delete derived class objects.
keyPoints: Without virtual destructor: only base destructor called
With virtual destructor: derived destructor then base destructor called
Program with base class having non-virtual destructor
Program with virtual destructor
Show output difference to demonstrate proper cleanup
Explain memory leak scenario without virtual destructor
When to make destructors virtual: class with virtual functions
Constructor cannot be virtual, explain why

questions.question: Explain RTTI (Run Time Type Information) in C++. Demonstrate the use of dynamic_cast with suitable examples.
keyPoints: RTTI provides runtime type identification
Enabled with virtual functions in class hierarchy
dynamic_cast for safe downcasting
Returns nullptr if cast fails (for pointers)
Throws bad_cast if fails (for references)
typeid operator returns type information
Example showing successful and failed dynamic_cast
Use cases: checking object type at runtime
Limitations: only works with polymorphic types
